description: The EU General Data Protection Regulation (Regulation (EU) 2016/679) has 99 Articles across 11 Chapters. This page covers Chapters II–IV — the ones that map most cleanly onto security/compliance controls: Principles, Rights of the Data Subject, and Controller/Processor obligations.

items: GDPR — the EU General Data Protection Regulation, in force since May 2018. A regulation, not a directive, so it applies directly and uniformly across all EU member states.

Applies based on **whose data you process**, not where your company is based — it covers any organization processing the personal data of people in the EU, regardless of where the organization itself is located.
Enforced by national **supervisory authorities** in each member state, with fines up to €20 million or 4% of global annual turnover, whichever is higher.

overview.title: Key Differences from Other Compliance Frameworks

blocks.text: GDPR vs SOC 2 / ISO 27001 / HIPAA

items: **GDPR** — a legal requirement (not voluntary) governing personal data of EU individuals specifically, with its own regulatory enforcement body per country.

**SOC 2 / ISO 27001** — voluntary security frameworks; can demonstrate some of the technical safeguards GDPR requires (Article 32), but don't cover GDPR's legal bases, consent, or data-subject-rights machinery.

**HIPAA** — a similar "legally mandatory once triggered" model, but scoped narrowly to U.S. health information, whereas GDPR covers all personal data broadly and applies globally based on whose data it is.

blocks.text: See [SOC 2's Privacy category](/frameworks/soc2/controls) for where the frameworks overlap most directly — SOC 2's optional Privacy criteria (P1.0–P8.0) were explicitly modeled to align with GDPR-style obligations.
Regulation vs Directive

GDPR is a **regulation**, meaning it's binding law in every EU member state without each country needing to pass its own implementing legislation — unlike a directive, which sets a goal but leaves the specific law-making to each country. This is why GDPR compliance requirements are largely consistent across the EU, even though enforcement (fines, investigations) happens at the national level.

items: **Chapter II — Principles (Articles 5–11)** — the foundational rules for lawful processing: what counts as a legal basis, conditions for consent, and rules for special categories of data.
**Chapter III — Rights of the Data Subject (Articles 12–23)** — the specific rights individuals have over their own data: access, rectification, erasure, portability, and more.
**Chapter IV — Controller and Processor (Articles 24–43)** — the operational obligations on organizations: security of processing, breach notification, DPIAs, and the Data Protection Officer role.

blocks.text: GDPR has 99 Articles across 11 Chapters total. Chapters II–IV are the ones that translate most directly into concrete compliance controls — the rest cover things like international data transfers (Ch. V), supervisory authorities (Ch. VI–VII), remedies and penalties (Ch. VIII), and specific processing situations (Ch. IX).
See the [Controls tab](/frameworks/gdpr/controls) for the specific Articles from Chapters II–IV, with plain-English notes on what each actually requires.
It's Not Optional

items: **Legal requirement** — if you process personal data of anyone in the EU, GDPR applies regardless of where your company is based.
**Serious penalties** — fines up to €20 million or 4% of global annual turnover, plus the reputational cost of a public enforcement action.

**Breach notification obligations** — a personal data breach triggers a 72-hour notification requirement to the supervisory authority (Article 33), and in some cases direct notification to affected individuals (Article 34).

blocks.text: Business Reasons Beyond the Legal Floor

items: **Customer trust** — EU customers and partners increasingly expect GDPR-aligned data handling as a baseline, even outside the EU.
**Global spillover** — many other privacy laws (CCPA, Brazil's LGPD, and others) were modeled on GDPR, so a solid GDPR program covers a lot of the same ground elsewhere.

blocks.text: Quick self-score across all three chapters. If you're answering "no" to more than half the questions in any chapter, prioritize it.

rows: Can you name the specific legal basis (Art. 6) for every category of personal data you process?
Where you rely on consent, is it freely given, specific, and as easy to withdraw as to give?
Do you collect only the personal data actually needed for the stated purpose?
If you process special category data (health, biometric, etc.), do you have an Article 9 exception documented?
Can you fulfill a data subject access or portability request within one month?
Can you actually delete an individual's data on request, including from backups and third parties?
Is your privacy notice clear about what's collected, why, and for how long?
Do you have technical and organizational measures appropriate to the risk (Art. 32)?
Do you have a process to detect and report a breach to the supervisory authority within 72 hours?
Do you maintain a Records of Processing Activities (RoPA) register (Art. 30)?
Do you run Data Protection Impact Assessments for high-risk processing (Art. 35)?
Do you have Article 28-compliant data processing agreements with every vendor handling personal data?
Have you assessed whether you're required to appoint a Data Protection Officer, and done so if needed?

blocks.text: Identifying Gaps

items: A Records of Processing Activities (RoPA) exercise is usually the fastest way to surface gaps — you can't assess what you haven't inventoried.
[The official GDPR text on EUR-Lex](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2016/679/oj) is the canonical source; most EU supervisory authorities also publish free practical guidance.

blocks.text: What Takes Longest

Operationalizing data subject rights — especially erasure and portability — consistently takes the longest, because it requires your systems (and often your vendors' systems) to actually support finding, exporting, and deleting one specific person's data on demand, not just having a policy that says you will.
What to Implement First

items: **A Records of Processing Activities (RoPA)** — the foundational inventory everything else depends on.
**A clear, accurate privacy notice** — required regardless of maturity level, and cheap to get right once the RoPA exists.
**Article 28 processor agreements** — an inventory-and-paperwork exercise, but one that's easy to let slip.
**A breach notification process** — the 72-hour clock doesn't wait for you to build this after the fact.

Once those are in place, move to the harder operational work — building real data subject rights fulfillment (access/erasure/portability) and a repeatable DPIA process — since those need system-level support, not just documentation.

blocks.text: Unlike SOC 2 or ISO 27001, there's no certification or audit cycle — GDPR compliance is demonstrated on an ongoing basis and tested when a regulator investigates.
Step-by-Step Process

items: **Map your data** — build the Records of Processing Activities register: what personal data you hold, why, and where it goes.
**Identify legal bases** — assign a specific Article 6 legal basis to every processing activity.
**Close technical/organizational gaps** — implement Article 32 security measures appropriate to the risk.
**Build rights-fulfillment processes** — access, rectification, erasure, portability, objection.
**Put processor agreements in place** — Article 28-compliant contracts with every vendor touching personal data.
**Establish breach response** — a process that can hit the 72-hour supervisory authority notification window.

blocks.text: GDPR doesn't have a voluntary third-party audit like SOC 2. Enforcement is driven by supervisory authorities, usually triggered by a breach, a complaint, or a proactive investigation.
How Enforcement Actually Happens

items: **Breach-triggered** — a reported breach can prompt a supervisory authority investigation into your broader compliance posture, not just the incident itself.
**Complaint-triggered** — any individual can lodge a complaint directly with a supervisory authority.
**Proactive investigations** — supervisory authorities run sector-wide sweeps and audits independent of any specific complaint.

blocks.text: Common Findings

Regulatory enforcement actions consistently point to the same gaps: no valid legal basis for processing, inadequate consent mechanisms, missing or superficial DPIAs for high-risk processing, and processor agreements that don't meet Article 28's requirements.
Compliance is continuous — there's no report to renew annually, but the underlying obligations apply to every new processing activity you start.
Ongoing Requirements

Keep the RoPA current as processing activities change, re-run DPIAs when high-risk processing changes materially, and review processor agreements whenever you onboard a new vendor touching personal data.
Scaling Compliance
New products or features that introduce new categories of personal data need a legal basis and a RoPA entry before launch, not after — and may trigger a fresh DPIA if the processing is high-risk.

categories.title: Principles
categories.summary: The foundational rules for lawful processing: legal bases, consent, and special category data (Articles 5–11).

controls.criteria: Principles relating to processing of personal data — lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, data minimization, accuracy, storage limitation, integrity/confidentiality, and accountability.
controls.notes: The seven core principles everything else in GDPR builds on. "Accountability" specifically means you must be able to demonstrate compliance, not just claim it.

controls.criteria: Lawfulness of processing — every processing activity needs one of six legal bases: consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, or legitimate interests.
controls.notes: Document which legal basis applies to each processing activity in your RoPA — "we've always done it this way" isn't a legal basis.

controls.criteria: Conditions for consent — where relied upon, consent must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous, and as easy to withdraw as to give.
controls.notes: A pre-ticked box or buried ToS clause doesn't meet this bar. Withdrawal needs to be a real, equally easy option.

controls.criteria: Conditions applicable to child's consent for information society services — parental consent generally required under age 16 (member states can lower to 13).
controls.notes: If your service could reasonably be used by minors, verify the applicable age threshold in each relevant member state.

controls.criteria: Processing of special categories of personal data — health, biometric, genetic, racial/ethnic origin, political opinions, religious beliefs, and similar data is prohibited by default, subject to specific exceptions.
controls.notes: If you process any of these categories, identify and document the specific Article 9 exception you're relying on — the bar is meaningfully higher than for ordinary personal data.

controls.criteria: Processing of personal data relating to criminal convictions and offences — only under official authority or specific legal authorization.
controls.notes: Treat this like special category data — most organizations shouldn't be processing it without a specific documented legal basis.

controls.criteria: Processing which does not require identification — controllers aren't obligated to maintain identifying information solely to enable GDPR rights if they don't otherwise need it.
controls.notes: If you've genuinely anonymized data, some rights (access, erasure) may not apply — but true anonymization is a high bar, not just removing a name field.

categories.title: Rights of the Data Subject
categories.summary: The specific rights individuals have over their own data — access, rectification, erasure, portability, and objection (Articles 12–23).

controls.criteria: Transparent information, communication and modalities — information provided to data subjects must be concise, transparent, intelligible, and easily accessible.
controls.notes: Legal-team-only privacy policies fail this standard. Write for the actual reader, not just for defensibility.

controls.criteria: Information to be provided at collection (Art. 13) or where data wasn't obtained directly from the subject (Art. 14) — what's collected, why, retention period, and rights available.
controls.notes: Your privacy notice needs to actually match what you do with data, reviewed whenever practices change.

controls.criteria: Right of access — data subjects can request confirmation of whether their data is processed, and a copy of it plus supporting details.
controls.notes: This is the core DSAR (data subject access request) right — you need a process and a defined SLA (one month, extendable) to fulfill it.

controls.criteria: Right to rectification — data subjects can have inaccurate personal data corrected.
controls.notes: A straightforward self-service or support-ticket mechanism for correcting data usually satisfies this.

controls.criteria: Right to erasure ('right to be forgotten') — data subjects can request deletion under specific conditions (e.g. data no longer necessary, consent withdrawn).
controls.notes: The hardest right to operationalize technically — deletion needs to actually propagate through backups, logs, and any third parties you've shared data with.

controls.criteria: Right to restriction of processing — data subjects can require processing to be paused (not deleted) under specific conditions, e.g. while accuracy is disputed.
controls.notes: Needs a technical way to flag a record as "restricted" without deleting it — different from erasure.

controls.criteria: Notification obligation regarding rectification, erasure, or restriction — controllers must notify each recipient the data was disclosed to, unless disproportionate effort.
controls.notes: Track who you've shared data with so you can actually notify them when a correction or deletion happens.

controls.criteria: Right to data portability — data subjects can receive their data in a structured, commonly used, machine-readable format, and transmit it to another controller.
controls.notes: Build an export function that produces something usable (JSON/CSV), not a PDF printout.

controls.criteria: Right to object — data subjects can object to processing based on legitimate interests or for direct marketing, and processing must stop unless compelling legitimate grounds override it.
controls.notes: For marketing specifically, an objection must be honored unconditionally and immediately.

controls.criteria: Automated individual decision-making, including profiling — data subjects generally have the right not to be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing that has legal or similarly significant effects.
controls.notes: If you use automated decisioning (e.g. algorithmic credit/hiring decisions), you need a human-review path and to disclose the logic involved.

controls.criteria: Restrictions — member states may restrict the scope of certain rights/obligations by law, for specific public-interest reasons (national security, defense, etc.).
controls.notes: Relevant mainly if you operate in a regulated sector where a member state has legislated a specific carve-out.

categories.title: Controller and Processor
categories.summary: The operational obligations on organizations: security, breach notification, DPIAs, and the DPO role (Articles 24–43).

controls.criteria: Responsibility of the controller — controllers must implement appropriate technical and organizational measures and be able to demonstrate compliance.
controls.notes: This is GDPR's accountability principle turned into an operational requirement — you need evidence, not just intent.

controls.criteria: Data protection by design and by default — privacy safeguards built into processing from the start, with data minimization as the default setting.
controls.notes: New features should default to collecting/retaining the minimum necessary, with broader collection an explicit opt-in, not the reverse.

controls.criteria: Joint controllers — where two or more controllers jointly determine the purposes and means of processing, they must transparently allocate responsibilities.
controls.notes: Document the arrangement in writing if you're processing data jointly with another organization, not just informally splitting duties.

controls.criteria: Representatives of controllers or processors not established in the EU — generally required to designate an EU representative.
controls.notes: If you're a non-EU company processing EU personal data at scale, you likely need a designated EU representative, not just a compliance policy.

controls.criteria: Processor — controllers may only use processors providing sufficient guarantees, governed by a written contract covering specific required terms.
controls.notes: Every vendor processing personal data on your behalf needs an Article 28-compliant Data Processing Agreement, not just a standard vendor contract.

controls.criteria: Processing under the authority of the controller or processor — anyone processing data under a controller/processor's authority may only do so on instructions.
controls.notes: Employee/contractor access to personal data should be scoped to documented instructions, not open-ended.

controls.criteria: Records of processing activities — controllers and processors (with some exceptions for small organizations) must maintain a written record of processing activities.
controls.notes: The RoPA — your foundational inventory of what personal data you process, why, and where it goes. Almost every other GDPR obligation traces back to this.

controls.criteria: Cooperation with the supervisory authority — controllers and processors must cooperate with the supervisory authority on request.
controls.notes: Have a defined internal process for responding to a regulator inquiry, rather than improvising if one arrives.

controls.criteria: Security of processing — appropriate technical and organizational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, considering the state of the art and cost of implementation.
controls.notes: This is GDPR's direct link to security controls like SOC 2/ISO 27001 — encryption, resilience, and regular testing of controls all fall under this article.

controls.criteria: Notification of a personal data breach to the supervisory authority — without undue delay, and where feasible, within 72 hours of becoming aware.
controls.notes: The 72-hour clock is one of GDPR's most operationally demanding requirements — you need a detection and escalation process fast enough to make the deadline.

controls.criteria: Communication of a personal data breach to the data subject — required when the breach is likely to result in a high risk to the individual's rights and freedoms.
controls.notes: A higher bar than Article 33's regulator notification — not every reportable breach requires notifying affected individuals directly.

controls.criteria: Data protection impact assessment (DPIA) — required for processing likely to result in high risk to individuals' rights and freedoms, particularly for new technologies.
controls.notes: Build a DPIA process triggered automatically for high-risk processing (large-scale special category data, systematic monitoring, new tech) rather than deciding case by case.

controls.criteria: Prior consultation — if a DPIA indicates high residual risk, the controller must consult the supervisory authority before processing begins.
controls.notes: A rare escalation path — most DPIAs should identify mitigations that avoid needing this, but the option (and obligation) exists.

controls.criteria: Designation, position, and tasks of the Data Protection Officer (DPO) — required for public authorities, large-scale systematic monitoring, or large-scale special category data processing.
controls.notes: Assess whether you're required to appoint a DPO; if so, they need genuine independence and direct reporting to top management, not a buried compliance function.

controls.criteria: Codes of conduct and certification mechanisms — industry bodies can develop approved codes of conduct, and certification schemes can help demonstrate compliance.
controls.notes: Voluntary industry mechanisms — worth watching if your sector develops an approved code of conduct, since adherence can support (though not guarantee) compliance.
